subject: Physics
description: Initial project proposal. The goal is to develop a machine learning-based workflow to automatically select optimal turbulence models for specific computational fluid dynamics (CFD) simulations, improving both accuracy and computational efficiency.
reason: This is the first event of the research project. It is logical to start with a 'proposal' to define the initial research goals and a high-level plan, which will guide the subsequent stages of the project.

subject: Physics
description: Initial design of the methodological scheme. This involves selecting a set of representative CFD benchmark cases (e.g., flow over a cylinder, backward-facing step), defining features to characterize these cases (e.g., Reynolds number, geometry parameters), and choosing a preliminary machine learning model architecture (e.g., a simple Random Forest classifier) to map features to the best turbulence model.
reason: Following the initial 'proposal' (e_01), the next logical step is to translate the high-level goals and plan into a concrete experimental and methodological design. This 'scheme_design' event creates the initial 'method_scheme' that will be tested in subsequent pilot experiments.

subject: Physics
description: Ran small-scale pilot experiments to validate the designed scheme. This involved running simulations for one benchmark case (flow over a cylinder) with a few turbulence models, extracting the defined features, and training the preliminary Random Forest model to check for basic feasibility.
reason: With an initial 'method_scheme' defined in e_02, the next logical step is to conduct 'pilot_experiments'. This serves as a crucial sanity check to ensure the proposed feature extraction and ML model are viable before committing resources to large-scale main experiments. This event generates the first 'experiment_results'.

subject: Physics
description: Analyzed the results from the pilot experiment. The analysis indicated that while the Random Forest model could distinguish between the turbulence models with some success, its accuracy was limited. The features based solely on global parameters like Reynolds number appear insufficient to capture the local flow physics crucial for model selection.
reason: After obtaining initial 'experiment_results' from the pilot study (e_03), a dedicated 'analysis' step is necessary to interpret the findings. This analysis evaluates the effectiveness of the initial 'method_scheme' against the 'research_goals' and identifies its shortcomings, which will guide the next iteration of the research cycle. The summary of this analysis forms a preliminary 'paper_paragraph'.

subject: Physics
description: Revising the methodological scheme based on the poor pilot results. The new design moves away from simple global features and proposes extracting local flow-field data (e.g., velocity gradients, pressure distributions) from precursor simulations to create a richer feature set. This aims to provide the model with the physical insights it was previously lacking.
reason: The previous 'analysis' (e_04) concluded that the initial 'method_scheme' was ineffective due to simplistic features. A 'scheme_design' revision is the logical next step to address this core methodological weakness before proceeding with further experiments. This event updates the 'method_scheme' based on insights from the pilot study.

subject: Physics
description: A second round of pilot experiments is conducted using the revised method. The experiment again uses the 'flow over a cylinder' case but now implements the new feature extraction pipeline to generate features from local flow-field data. The goal is to verify if these richer features improve the model's predictive performance as hypothesized.
reason: After revising the 'method_scheme' in e_05 to address the shortcomings identified in the first pilot study, the logical next step is to test this new scheme. Running another 'pilot_experiments' phase is a cost-effective way to validate the more complex feature extraction process before scaling up to main experiments, generating new 'experiment_results' for analysis.

subject: Physics
description: Analysis of the second round of pilot experiments. The results show that using local flow-field data as features significantly improves the model's predictive accuracy, achieving over 90% success in selecting the optimal turbulence model for the cylinder case. This confirms the new approach is viable.
reason: Following the second pilot experiment (e_06) with the revised method, a thorough 'analysis' is required to evaluate its effectiveness. The positive outcome of this analysis validates the new 'method_scheme' and provides the confidence needed to proceed to the main, larger-scale experiments. The findings are summarized as new 'experiment_results' and a 'paper_paragraph' for the methods section.

subject: Physics
description: Begin the main experimental campaign. This involves running the full set of CFD simulations for all selected benchmark cases (including backward-facing step, airfoil, etc.), applying the validated feature extraction pipeline to generate the comprehensive dataset, and training the ML model on this larger dataset.
reason: After the successful validation of the revised methodology in the second pilot study (e_07), the project is ready to scale up. Moving to 'main_experiments' is the logical next step to collect the comprehensive data across multiple benchmark cases needed to fully address the research goals.

subject: Physics
description: A comprehensive analysis of the main experiment results. The analysis confirms high accuracy across most cases but reveals that the model struggles with simulations involving strong shockwaves, as the current features do not adequately capture shock physics.
reason: Following the large-scale data generation from the 'main_experiments' (e_08), a detailed 'analysis' is crucial to assess the overall performance of the workflow. This step synthesizes the findings, validates the approach against the 'research_goals', and identifies specific limitations (like handling shockwaves) that will guide future refinements.

subject: Physics
description: Began drafting the full research paper. The 'Methods' section is written based on the successful revised scheme, and the 'Results' section details the high accuracy achieved in the main experiments. The limitation concerning flows with strong shockwaves is noted and framed as a direction for future work in the discussion section.
reason: After the comprehensive analysis of the main experiments in e_09, the project has produced significant, publishable results. It is a logical next step to begin the 'writing' phase to consolidate the findings from the 'experiment_results' and 'method_scheme' into a manuscript, which also serves to formally document the project's progress and limitations.

subject: Physics
description: After drafting the initial results, the team decides the shockwave limitation is too significant to defer. The project goals are officially revised to include the development of a robust workflow that is also accurate for compressible, high-Mach number flows, not just the initially planned incompressible cases.
reason: The analysis in e_09 and writing in e_10 revealed a major limitation. Instead of publishing a paper with a known weakness, the team makes a strategic decision to expand the project's scope. Revisiting the 'proposal' to update the 'research_goals' formalizes this new ambition and provides a clear mandate for the subsequent methodological revisions that will be required.

subject: Physics
description: Third iteration of the scheme design, specifically to address the newly expanded goals. The method is updated to include features sensitive to compressibility effects, such as local Mach number and density gradients, which are hypothesized to help the model identify and correctly handle regions with shockwaves.
reason: Following the revision of 'research_goals' in e_11 to include high-Mach flows, the existing 'method_scheme' is now inadequate. This 'scheme_design' event is the necessary next step to adapt the methodology, using insights from previous 'experiment_results' (e_09) to engineer new features capable of handling the physics of shockwaves.

subject: Physics
description: A new round of pilot experiments is run to test the third iteration of the method. This involves simulating a compressible, high-Mach number flow case (e.g., transonic flow over an airfoil) to generate a strong shockwave. The goal is to verify if the new compressibility-sensitive features allow the model to perform well in this challenging regime.
reason: After revising the 'method_scheme' in e_12 to handle high-Mach flows as per the updated 'research_goals' (e_11), running a targeted 'pilot_experiments' is the necessary next step. This serves as a critical validation for the new features before committing to another expensive main experimental campaign, following the project's established iterative development cycle.

subject: Physics
description: Analysis of the high-Mach number pilot experiment. The results are promising: the new compressibility-sensitive features significantly improve the model's ability to select appropriate turbulence models in the presence of shockwaves. However, performance degrades slightly for very strong, detached shocks, suggesting a minor refinement might still be beneficial.
reason: After conducting a targeted pilot experiment (e_13) for the expanded research goals, a dedicated 'analysis' step is essential. This event evaluates the success of the revised 'method_scheme' from e_12, providing the critical verdict on whether the new features work as intended before committing to a final set of main experiments.

subject: Physics
description: A final, minor refinement of the method scheme to address the issue with very strong shocks. Based on the analysis in e_14, the feature set is augmented with an additional metric specifically designed to quantify shock strength and detachment distance, aiming to improve robustness in extreme compressible flow regimes.
reason: The previous analysis (e_14) revealed a minor but specific weakness in the method's handling of strong, detached shocks. This targeted 'scheme_design' refinement is a logical and low-cost step to improve the method's robustness before committing to the final, large-scale main experiments for the expanded project scope.

subject: Physics
description: Running the final, comprehensive main experiments. This campaign uses the fully refined method to simulate the entire suite of benchmark cases, including the original incompressible flows and the challenging high-Mach number cases with strong shocks, to generate a definitive and robust dataset.
reason: After a final, targeted refinement of the 'method_scheme' in e_15, the next logical step is to execute the 'main_experiments' for the expanded project scope. This will generate the final, comprehensive 'experiment_results' needed to validate the workflow's performance across all intended flow regimes and provide the core data for the final paper.

subject: Physics
description: Final, comprehensive analysis of the complete dataset from the main experiments. The analysis confirms that the fully refined workflow performs with high accuracy across all benchmark cases, including the challenging high-Mach number flows with strong, detached shocks, successfully meeting the expanded research goals.
reason: After completing the final main experimental campaign in e_16, a comprehensive 'analysis' is the mandatory next step. This event serves to definitively validate the final 'method_scheme' against the expanded 'research_goals' and to synthesize the conclusive findings, which form the basis for the final paper.

subject: Physics
description: A major revision of the research paper is undertaken. The 'Methods' section is updated to include the final feature set for compressible flows, and the 'Results' section is completely rewritten to present the comprehensive findings from the final main experiments, demonstrating the workflow's robustness across all test cases.
reason: With the final analysis (e_17) confirming the project's success in meeting its expanded goals, the next logical step is to finalize the manuscript. This 'writing' event integrates all the conclusive findings, methodological refinements, and results into a coherent and complete research paper, preparing it for submission.

subject: Physics
description: The project concludes with the submission of the finalized manuscript to a high-impact fluid mechanics journal. The research successfully developed, validated, and documented a novel workflow for turbulence model selection across a wide range of flow regimes, fulfilling all revised research objectives.
reason: The project has reached a natural conclusion. The methodological scheme is stable and validated (e_17), the main experimental results are comprehensive and conclusive (e_17), and the research paper has been substantially written and revised (e_18). Submitting the paper marks the completion of the core research cycle.
